article.source: RocketNews | Top News Stories From Around the Globe
article.body_preview: Dhaka, Bangladesh - Spice trader Mohammed Enayet Ullah has made at least four attempts since November to open a letter of credit to pay for imports of cumin, cardamom and cloves, some of the most essential spices used in Bangladeshi cooking, only to be refused by banks due to a shortage of dollars.Importers in Bangladesh need to open letters of credit with one of the country's 61 scheduled banks to buy foreign goods and services. It is essentially a financial contract issued by an importer's ban

llm_connections.evidence_quote: Importers in Bangladesh need to open letters of credit with one of the country's 61 scheduled banks to buy foreign goods and services.
llm_connections.scope_justification: The article states at the national level that importers in Bangladesh must open letters of credit to buy foreign goods, matching the feature's country-wide imports scope driven by LC availability.

verification.quote_best_match: dollars.Importers in Bangladesh need to open letters of credit with one of the country's 61 scheduled banks to buy foreign goods and services.
verification.judge_reasoning: The article contains the sentence verbatim that importers in Bangladesh must open letters of credit to buy foreign goods and services, which explicitly links LCs as a required mechanism enabling imports; the statement is made at the national (Bangladesh) level.
